summary: This PR adds PathLike support to `send_file` and `send_from_directory` via a `_compat.fspath` shim and updates both helpers and tests accordingly. The main concerns are: (1) the compat `fspath` fallback doesn't enforce PEP 519 type semantics and silently accepts arbitrary objects, and (2) bytes-returning `__fspath__` implementations could reach `safe_join` and bypass or break directory-traversal checks. Style and test-coverage gaps are minor but worth addressing.

findings.title: Fallback `fspath` doesn't validate input type, silently accepts arbitrary objects
findings.detail: The PEP 519 backwards-compatibility shim returns `path` unchanged whenever the object lacks `__fspath__`. The real `os.fspath()` raises `TypeError` for anything that is not a `str`, `bytes`, or `os.PathLike`. The permissive fallback silently forwards integers, `None`, dicts, and any other arbitrary objects into `safe_join` / `os.path.join`, where the resulting error message will obscure the actual problem. While not a direct injection risk, it weakens the input-validation contract throughout `send_file` and `send_from_directory`. The fallback should at minimum add `isinstance(path, (str, bytes))` and raise `TypeError` otherwise, matching CPython's reference implementation from PEP 519.

findings.title: bytes path from `__fspath__` can bypass or break `safe_join` traversal check
findings.detail: PEP 519 permits `__fspath__()` to return either `str` or `bytes`. If it returns `bytes`, the result is passed to `safe_join`, which operates on strings. Depending on the Werkzeug version, this either raises an unhelpful `TypeError` (bypassing the expected `BadRequest` error handling) or produces undefined behaviour. On the fallback Python 2 code path, the custom `fspath()` also returns bytes objects unchanged. A bytes path containing a null byte (`b'/etc/passwd\x00'`) could truncate to an arbitrary path on older C-extension layers. Adding an explicit `isinstance(result, str)` check (or raising `ValueError`/`TypeError` for bytes results) after every `fspath()` call in `send_file` and `send_from_directory` would close this gap. The `fspath` calls on both `directory` (line 715) and `filename` (line 716) are independently affected.

findings.title: Typo: "as describe" should be "as described"
findings.detail: The changelog entry reads "supports `PathLike` objects as describe in PEP 0519" — "describe" should be "described".

findings.title: Redundant `hasattr` guard before calling `fspath` in `send_file`
findings.detail: The `send_file` function checks `hasattr(filename_or_fp, '__fspath__')` before calling `fspath(filename_or_fp)`, but `send_from_directory` (lines 715–716) calls `fspath` unconditionally — consistent with the PEP 519 spec and the compat shim, which both handle non-PathLike objects transparently. The extra guard is redundant and introduces a subtle inconsistency: a bytes `filename_or_fp` without `__fspath__` skips the `fspath()` call entirely and falls into the `else` branch, treating a plain bytes path as a file object. Calling `fspath` unconditionally (and then checking `isinstance(result, string_types)`) would simplify the logic, match the style of `send_from_directory`, and make the bytes-file-object distinction explicit. Per PEP 519, `__fspath__` returning a non-str/bytes value is not enforced here either — an `isinstance` check after the call would harden this path. (Reviewers note: on the current Python 2 fallback the logic is coincidentally correct, making this a style/consistency issue with a latent correctness risk rather than an active bug.)

findings.title: Fallback `fspath` implementation not tested in isolation (neither branch)
findings.detail: Tests use `FakePath` (which has `__fspath__`) but always run against whichever branch is imported at test time. Neither the `__fspath__`-present branch nor the plain-string/bytes pass-through branch of the fallback implementation is exercised directly. There is also no test passing a PathLike whose `__fspath__` returns `bytes` rather than `str`. If the fallback is accidentally broken, no test will catch it. Direct unit tests of the fallback `fspath` function with both a PathLike object and a plain string (and ideally a bytes value) would close this gap.

findings.title: PathLike `send_file` test only covers relative path — absolute path case missing
findings.detail: `send_file` has separate branches for relative filenames (joined against `root_path`) and absolute filenames (`os.path.isabs`). `test_send_file_pathlike` only exercises the relative case (`FakePath('static/index.html')`). A test passing an absolute `PathLike` path would verify the `hasattr`/`fspath` conversion works for the absolute branch as well.

findings.title: `send_from_directory` PathLike test only covers both-args-PathLike; mixed cases not tested
findings.detail: `test_send_from_directory_pathlike` wraps both `directory` and `filename` in `FakePath`. The `fspath()` calls on lines 715–716 are independent, so a regression that breaks only one of them would not be caught. Adding tests with `FakePath` for `directory` and a plain string for `filename`, and vice versa, would provide the necessary coverage.

findings.title: PathLike static-file test appended inline rather than as its own test method
findings.detail: The surrounding test methods don't use inline section comments inside test functions; each scenario is its own test method. The new block at lines 706–710 is appended inside `test_static_file` with a plain comment. For consistency with `test_send_file_pathlike` and `test_send_from_directory_pathlike` (also added in this PR), this should be its own `test_static_file_pathlike` method.

findings.title: Compat `fspath` body style inconsistent with surrounding functions
findings.detail: The existing compat functions (`reraise`, `implements_to_string`, `with_metaclass`) all use conventional multi-line bodies. The new `fspath` shim places its entire body on the same line as `def`, which is inconsistent with the surrounding style. Expanding to a standard multi-line `if/else` or early-return form would match the file's conventions.

findings.title: Changelog PEP citation style inconsistent with surrounding entries
findings.detail: All other changelog entries use only the `(#NNNN_)` reference format at the end of the bullet; none embed an inline PEP citation in the sentence body. The new entry puts "PEP 0519" inline as prose and still ends with `(#3059_)`. For consistency the PEP mention could be dropped or moved to a parenthetical, matching the surrounding style.

findings.title: Docstring backtick formatting for "PathLike object" is non-standard
findings.detail: The docstring reads "a ``PathLike object``". RST inline-code markers wrap the space and "object" into the formatted span. For clarity and consistency with how the rest of the file refers to Python concepts (e.g. ``pathlib.Path``), this could be written as "a :class:`os.PathLike` object" or simply "a PathLike object" (no backticks around the generic noun).

findings.title: Fallback `fspath` doesn't validate the type of path for plain strings/bytes
findings.detail: The PEP 519 backwards-compatibility shim simply returns `path` unchanged when it lacks `__fspath__`. The real `os.fspath()` raises a `TypeError` if the argument is not a `str`, `bytes`, or `os.PathLike`. The fallback silently accepts anything (integers, None, arbitrary objects) and returns it as-is, which can mask programming errors downstream instead of failing loudly. The fallback should at least check `isinstance(path, (str, bytes))` before returning and raise `TypeError` otherwise, matching `os.fspath` semantics.

findings.title: `send_file` uses `hasattr(filename_or_fp, '__fspath__')` instead of calling `fspath()` unconditionally
findings.detail: The guard `hasattr(filename_or_fp, '__fspath__')` duplicates the logic that already exists inside `fspath()`. Since `fspath()` is a no-op for plain strings/bytes, the call can simply be `filename_or_fp = fspath(filename_or_fp)` without the `hasattr` check. More critically, the fallback `fspath` in `_compat.py` (Python 2 path) also just returns non-PathLike objects unchanged, so the `hasattr` check is redundant and adds an inconsistency: if someone passes a type that defines `__fspath__` returning bytes (valid in Python 3), the guard will trigger `fspath()` correctly, but for a bytes path without `__fspath__` it goes straight through. This is a minor inconsistency rather than an outright bug, but the inconsistency could mask issues (e.g., a bytes filename_or_fp that isn't a string_type would fall into the `else` branch and be treated as a file object). The real correctness risk is that on Python 2, `fspath` returns whatever it receives, so a PathLike object that isn't a string would pass the `hasattr` check, call `__fspath__`, but the returned value might be a unicode/bytes object that is still correctly handled downstream — so the logic is coincidentally correct.

findings.title: In `send_from_directory`, `fspath(filename)` result is passed to `safe_join` after being converted to a string, but `safe_join` receives the raw `directory` string too — order matters
findings.detail: `fspath` is called on both `filename` and `directory` before `safe_join`. This is correct. However, if `fspath(filename)` returns bytes (which is legal for `os.fspath` in Python 3), `safe_join` — which uses string operations internally — would fail. This is an edge case: `os.fspath` can return `bytes` if `__fspath__` returns bytes, and the code does nothing to reject that or convert it. The fallback `fspath` in `_compat.py` will also return bytes if `__fspath__` returns bytes, and `safe_join` will then receive a bytes object for the filename parameter. This will likely raise a `TypeError` inside `safe_join` rather than a clear `BadRequest`, breaking the expected error handling.

findings.title: Custom fspath() fallback silently accepts arbitrary non-path objects
findings.detail: The fallback `fspath()` implementation returns `path` unchanged when the object has no `__fspath__` attribute, matching PEP 519 intent. However, PEP 519 specifies that `os.fspath()` should raise `TypeError` for objects that are neither `str`, `bytes`, nor `os.PathLike`. The permissive fallback means callers can pass arbitrary objects (e.g. dicts, integers) without any error being raised on Python 2 or older Python 3 environments — the object is silently forwarded into `safe_join` / `os.path.join`, where the resulting error message may obscure the actual problem. While not a direct injection risk, it weakens the input-validation contract in `send_file` and `send_from_directory`. Consider adding `isinstance` checks (str/bytes) consistent with the CPython reference implementation in the PEP.

findings.title: PathLike check in send_file uses duck-typing instead of os.fspath(), allowing __fspath__ to return non-string
findings.detail: The code checks `hasattr(filename_or_fp, '__fspath__')` and then calls `fspath()`. Per PEP 519, `__fspath__()` must return `str` or `bytes`, but this is not enforced here. If a malicious or broken `__fspath__` returns a non-string (e.g. another path-like object, a bytes subclass carrying a null byte), the resulting value is passed directly into `os.path.isabs` and the file-serving pipeline without further sanitisation. The `safe_join` call downstream in `send_from_directory` does guard against directory traversal for string paths, but bytes paths or unexpected types could bypass it. Ensuring `fspath()` result is `str` (via `isinstance` check after calling `fspath`) before use would harden this path.

findings.title: fspath() called unconditionally on directory and filename before safe_join — bytes path could bypass safe_join traversal check
findings.detail: `fspath(filename)` and `fspath(directory)` are called unconditionally. If the PathLike object's `__fspath__` returns `bytes` (which is valid per PEP 519), `safe_join` will receive bytes arguments. `werkzeug.security.safe_join` (at the time of this Flask version) operates on strings; passing bytes may cause it to raise or, depending on the version, produce undefined behaviour. More critically, on the fallback Python 2 code path the custom `fspath()` returns the object unchanged if it has no `__fspath__`, so a bytes object could silently pass through. A bytes path containing a null byte (`b'/etc/passwd\x00'`) may truncate to an arbitrary path on some older C-extension layers. Adding an explicit `isinstance(result, str)` assertion (or rejecting bytes) after `fspath()` conversion would close this gap.

findings.title: Typo: "as describe" should be "as described"
findings.detail: The changelog entry reads "supports ``PathLike`` objects as describe in PEP 0519" — "describe" should be "described".

findings.title: Changelog reference style inconsistency: backtick-link vs. plain-text PEP number
findings.detail: All other changelog entries in the file use a `(`#NNNN`_)` reference format at the end of the bullet, with no inline PEP citation in the sentence body. The new entry puts the PEP number inline as prose ("PEP 0519") and still ends with `(`#3059`_)`. For consistency the PEP mention could be dropped or moved to a parenthetical, matching the surrounding style.

findings.title: Redundant `hasattr` guard before calling `fspath`
findings.detail: The `send_file` function checks `hasattr(filename_or_fp, '__fspath__')` before calling `fspath(filename_or_fp)`, but `send_from_directory` (lines 715-716) calls `fspath` unconditionally, as does the PEP 519 spec (the compat shim also handles non-PathLike objects). Using `fspath` unconditionally in both places would be simpler and more consistent with the style used in `send_from_directory`. The extra `hasattr` guard adds complexity without benefit.

findings.title: Compat `fspath` function body on one line differs from multi-line style of surrounding functions
findings.detail: The existing compat functions in this file (`reraise`, `implements_to_string`, `with_metaclass`) all use multi-line bodies. The new `fspath` shim puts its entire body on the same line as `def`, which is inconsistent. Expanding to a conventional multi-line `if/else` or early-return form would match the surrounding style.

findings.title: Docstring says "a ``PathLike object``" — backticks wrap only the noun, not the full term
findings.detail: The docstring reads "a ``PathLike object``". The double backticks are RST inline-code markers, so the space and "object" are part of the formatted span. For clarity and consistency with how the rest of the docstrings in this file refer to Python concepts (e.g. ``pathlib.Path``), this could be written as "a :class:`os.PathLike` object" or simply "a PathLike object".

findings.title: Inline comment "# Test with static file handler." is not in the style of surrounding test code
findings.detail: The surrounding test methods don't use inline section comments inside test functions; each scenario is generally its own test method. The new block at lines 706-710 is appended inside `test_static_file` with a plain comment to distinguish it. For consistency with the rest of the test class, this could be its own `test_static_file_pathlike` method (analogous to `test_send_file_pathlike` and `test_send_from_directory_pathlike` also added in this PR).

findings.title: Fallback `fspath` implementation not tested in isolation
findings.detail: The `try/except ImportError` block provides a pure-Python fallback `fspath()` for Python 2 / environments without `os.fspath`. The tests use `FakePath` (which has `__fspath__`) but always run against whichever branch is imported at test time. There is no test that exercises the fallback path directly — e.g. calling the local `fspath` definition with an object that has `__fspath__`, and separately with a plain string (the `else` branch of `hasattr(path, '__fspath__')`). If someone accidentally breaks the fallback, no test will catch it.

findings.title: PathLike `send_file` only tested with a relative path — absolute path case not covered
findings.detail: `send_file` has two branches for string filenames: relative (goes through `root_path` join) and absolute (`os.path.isabs`). `test_send_file_pathlike` only exercises the relative path case (`FakePath('static/index.html')`). There is no test passing an absolute `PathLike` path to `send_file` to verify the `hasattr/__fspath__` conversion also works for the absolute branch.

findings.title: `send_from_directory` PathLike: only both-args-PathLike case tested; mixed (one PathLike, one str) not covered
findings.detail: `test_send_from_directory_pathlike` wraps both `directory` and `filename` in `FakePath`. The `fspath()` calls on lines 715-716 are independent, so a regression where only one of them is broken would not be caught. A test with `FakePath` for `directory` and a plain string for `filename` (and vice-versa) would close this gap.

findings.title: Fallback `fspath` plain-string (non-PathLike) pass-through branch not tested
findings.detail: The fallback `fspath` implementation returns `path` unchanged when `path` has no `__fspath__` attribute (i.e. it is already a plain string or bytes). No test calls `send_file` or `send_from_directory` with a `PathLike` object whose `__fspath__` returns bytes rather than str, nor tests the plain-value pass-through of the fallback directly.
